items.title: Integer overflow in allocation
items.description: Multiplication or addition of attacker-controlled values used as arguments to malloc/realloc without overflow checks. On 32-bit or with uint64→size_t truncation this can yield undersized buffers. See CWE-190.

items.title: Signed/unsigned integer confusion
items.description: Signed integers used where unsigned values are expected (or vice-versa), leading to unexpected comparison results, negative array indices, or wrap-around allocation sizes. See CWE-195, CWE-196.

items.title: Cross-architecture size_t truncation
items.description: uint64_t values narrowed to size_t or int on 32-bit builds, silently truncating high bits. Typically manifests in archive/parser code that reads 64-bit length fields. See CWE-681.

items.title: Use-after-free (UAF)
items.description: Memory is freed but a dangling pointer is later dereferenced, allowing heap metadata corruption or type confusion. See CWE-416.

items.title: Double free
items.description: The same heap block is passed to free() more than once, corrupting allocator metadata and potentially leading to arbitrary write primitives. See CWE-415.

items.title: Use-after-realloc pointer invalidation
items.description: A pointer to a heap block is cached before calling realloc(); on reallocation the block may move, leaving the old pointer dangling. See CWE-416.

items.title: Heap buffer overflow (write)
items.description: Writing past the end of a heap allocation, overwriting adjacent metadata or object fields. Often caused by off-by-one errors or missing length checks in copy loops. See CWE-122.

items.title: Stack buffer overflow
items.description: Writing beyond the bounds of a stack-allocated buffer, overwriting return addresses or saved registers. Classic in functions using strcpy, sprintf without bounds checking. See CWE-121.

items.title: Stack clash / large VLA exhaustion
items.description: Variable-length arrays (VLAs) or large stack allocations can collide with the heap on systems without guard pages, enabling control flow hijack. See CWE-1325.

items.title: Uninitialized memory read
items.description: Reading from memory before it has been initialized can leak sensitive data or allow control of program state. Common in structs with padding or conditionally initialized fields. See CWE-457.

items.title: Out-of-bounds read
items.description: Reading past the end of a buffer leaks adjacent memory, which can be used to defeat ASLR or exfiltrate sensitive data. See CWE-125.

items.title: Format string injection
items.description: User-controlled strings passed directly to printf-family functions allow arbitrary read/write through format specifiers. See CWE-134.

items.title: Signal handler async-signal-unsafe calls
items.description: Signal handlers that call non-reentrant functions (malloc, printf, exit, non-async-signal-safe syscalls) can corrupt program state when interrupted at the wrong time. Classic regreSSHion-class issue. See CWE-479.

items.title: Data race / lock ordering violation
items.description: Shared data accessed from multiple threads without synchronization, or locks acquired in inconsistent order leading to deadlock or TOCTOU races. See CWE-362, CWE-833.

items.title: Inconsistent cleanup on error paths
items.description: Resources (memory, file descriptors, locks) allocated before an error condition are not freed in all error exit paths, leading to leaks, UAF, or double-free. See CWE-401, CWE-772.

items.title: Unchecked malloc/realloc return
items.description: On allocation failure, malloc returns NULL. Dereferencing the result without checking leads to a null pointer dereference crash. On 64-bit systems the OS guard page prevents exploitation but can still cause DoS. See CWE-476.

items.title: Parser complexity / deeply nested input
items.description: Recursive parsers without depth limits can be forced into deep recursion by crafted input, causing stack exhaustion. See CWE-674.

items.title: Bytecode / binary format validation bypass
items.description: VM or bytecode engines that skip operand validation when loading pre-compiled data, enabling OOB register/constant-pool access or arbitrary jump targets. Proven high-probability pattern for IoT/embedded VMs.
